book.title: Stock Market Wizards: Interviews with America's Top Stock Traders
book.author: Jack D. Schwager

strategies.name: Blessed Stock Momentum
entry_rules: Identify stocks that are 'blessed' by the market - stocks that go up on good news AND hold up on bad news
Stock must show relative linearity (steady uptrend without wild swings)
Verify strong fundamental catalyst (earnings growth, new product, sector tailwind)
Confirm stock is difficult to buy (orders fill above limit price, showing demand)
Wait for stock to respond positively to first piece of negative news (proves institutional support)
exit_rules: Exit when stock fails to rally on good news (blessing has been removed)
Exit when stock breaks down on negative news it previously would have shrugged off
Exit immediately if macro environment shifts against the sector
Willingness to buy back at higher prices if setup reasserts
risk_management: Adapt strategy to current market environment (momentum stocks in momentum markets, value in value markets)
Keep a small separate 'gambling account' for tip-based trades to avoid contaminating main portfolio
Work alone to avoid influence from others' opinions
Take trading hiatus during personal difficulties (divorce, health issues)
strategies.source_chapter: Stuart Walton - Back from the Abyss
strategies.notes: Walton's approach is 25% fundamentals, 25% technical, 25% news response, 25% gut feel. Highly discretionary. Key insight: how a stock responds to news is more important than the news itself. Lost entire stake twice before succeeding. Emphasis on flexibility - 'float like a jellyfish'.

strategies.name: Deep Value Six-Screen Process
entry_rules: Screen 1 - Industry Knowledge: Identify industry with which you have deep familiarity
Screen 2 - Price Decline: Stock must have declined 50%+ from highs (market-adjusted)
Screen 3 - Strong Balance Sheet: Low debt, healthy cash flow, adequate liquidity
Screen 4 - Insider Buying: Significant insider purchases relative to salary/net worth
Screen 5 - Compelling Value: P/E below market, price near book value, price/cash flow attractive
Screen 6 - Identifiable Catalyst: What specific event will make this stock go up? (earnings turnaround, restructuring, new management, sector rotation, share buyback)
exit_rules: Target: 100% gain (double your money) within 12 months
Exit if catalyst fails to materialize or is negated
Exit if market fails to respond to anticipated positive catalyst
Upgrade portfolio: replace holdings with better risk/reward opportunities even if current position still has upside
risk_management: Maximum 15 concentrated positions
Never use leverage (learned from 1987 crash margin call)
Screens 1-5 are defensive (capital preservation); Screen 6 is offensive (profit generation)
Short positions held only 1-2 weeks as short-term trades with tight stops
Value alone is not sufficient - catalyst is mandatory
strategies.source_chapter: Michael Lauer - The Longs and Shorts of It
strategies.notes: One of the most codifiable strategies in the book. The six-screen process is systematic and replicable. Key insight: 'This business is not about investing in great companies, it's about profiting from inefficiently priced stocks.' Anti-diversification: concentrated portfolio beats index. Warns against leverage after 1987 crash wiped out gains despite correct stock picks.

strategies.name: Small Cap Value with Communication Research
entry_rules: Screen for stocks with P/E ratios of 8-12x (value territory)
Identify catalyst through extensive communication: call CFO, competitors, distributors, customers
Insider buying must be present as confirmation
Company must have identifiable reason to move higher in near term
Apply 'pig-at-the-trough' philosophy: constantly seek better opportunities
exit_rules: Replace current holdings with better risk/reward stocks (continuous portfolio upgrade)
Exit if catalyst is negated or fails to materialize
Exit if stock responds poorly to good news
Summarize reason for holding in 4 sentences or fewer (Peter Lynch rule) - if you can't, sell
risk_management: 100+ positions for diversification in illiquid small caps
Maximum 3% per position
Short positions: 20-30% stop losses, focus on high-P/E one-product companies
Must be willing to take losses - 'people who are successful are people willing to lose money'
strategies.source_chapter: Steve Watson - Dialing for Dollars
strategies.notes: Watson's edge is primary research via phone calls - calling companies, competitors, distributors, and customers directly. This 'boots on the ground' approach finds catalysts before Wall Street. Short selling targets: high-priced, single-product companies where product doesn't live up to claims or has no barrier to entry. Key insight: common sense research (trying the product, visiting stores) often beats Wall Street analysis.

strategies.name: Pure Short Selling - Fundamental Deterioration
entry_rules: Identify red flags: (1) High receivables, (2) Auditor changes, (3) CFO turnover, (4) Company blaming shorts for decline, (5) Company changing core business to chase hot trend
Stock must have very high P/E ratio
Identifiable catalyst for near-term decline (earnings deterioration, accounting issues, product failure)
Uptrend has stalled or reversed (price action confirmation)
All three conditions must be met: high P/E + catalyst + stalling price
exit_rules: Cover at support levels (take partial profits at technical levels)
Cover 1/3 of position at 20% loss
Cover if stock rallies on news you expected to be bearish
Never fight the market - if the news is out and stock doesn't drop, cover
risk_management: 50-60 short positions for diversification
Maximum 3% of portfolio per stock
Cover 1/3 of any position at 20% adverse move
Maintain diversification across sectors
Recognize that the long-term upward bias of markets works against shorts
strategies.source_chapter: Dana Galante - Against the Current
strategies.notes: One of the clearest short-selling frameworks in the book. Key insight: 'A good company could be a bad stock and vice versa.' Red flags are highly specific and verifiable. The three-part entry condition (high P/E + catalyst + stalling price) prevents premature shorting. Galante warns against shorting based solely on valuation - you need a catalyst for timing.

strategies.name: Cumulative Tick Oscillator - S&P Day Trading
entry_rules: Track NYSE cumulative tick throughout the day
Oversold signal: Cumulative tick falls below 5th percentile of historical readings
Overbought signal: Cumulative tick rises above 95th percentile of historical readings
IMPORTANT: Extreme high tick readings are SELL signals (opposite of conventional wisdom)
Extreme low tick readings are BUY signals
exit_rules: Profit target hit (relatively small targets)
Time stop: exit if target not reached within defined time window
Use profit objectives - don't try to squeeze maximum from each trade
Accept low return-per-trade in exchange for high probability
risk_management: Require 75%+ probability of winning before taking any trade
Low risk/reward ratio per trade compensated by very high win rate
Tape 'GET SMALLER' note to monitor - reduce size when losing
Stop trading during personal difficulties
Keep detailed daily diary of market observations

indicators.name: NYSE Cumulative Tick
indicators.parameters: Running sum of uptick minus downtick stocks

strategies.source_chapter: Mark D. Cook - Harvesting S&P Profits
strategies.notes: Cook's key discovery: everyone told him high tick = bullish, but his own research proved the opposite. Lesson: verify everything yourself. The cumulative tick is built from daily observation journals kept for years. Cook went bankrupt due to leverage before succeeding. Critical insight: the process of keeping a daily market diary was instrumental to developing all his strategies.

strategies.name: Conjunction Trade
entry_rules: NYSE Tick drops below -400
AND NYSE TIKI (Dow 30 tick) drops below -22
Both conditions must occur simultaneously (conjunction)
Buy signal on conjunction
exit_rules: Time stop: 21 minutes maximum hold
Profit target: 3 S&P points
Stop loss: 6 S&P points
risk_management: Strict time limit prevents overstaying
Defined profit target and stop loss before entry
Only take when probability exceeds 75%
strategies.source_chapter: Mark D. Cook - Harvesting S&P Profits
strategies.notes: One of the most specific and codifiable strategies in the entire book. Clear entry conditions, defined time limit, specific profit target and stop. The 21-minute time stop is unusual and data-driven. Risk/reward is 2:1 against (3pt target vs 6pt stop) but compensated by very high win rate.

strategies.name: Bond Ratio Trade
entry_rules: Monitor the ratio between S&P movement and bond movement
When S&P and bonds diverge from their typical relationship, trade the convergence
Specific ratio thresholds not disclosed in interview
exit_rules: Convergence of the ratio back to normal
Time stop if divergence persists
risk_management: Same framework as other Cook trades: 75%+ probability required
strategies.source_chapter: Mark D. Cook - Harvesting S&P Profits
strategies.notes: Mentioned but not detailed. The concept of intermarket divergence between stocks and bonds for day trading is sound but specifics are lacking.

strategies.name: Catapult Trade
entry_rules: Specific pattern identified by Cook through years of market observation
Details not fully disclosed in interview
exit_rules: Specific profit targets and time stops (not disclosed)
risk_management: 75%+ win probability required
strategies.source_chapter: Mark D. Cook - Harvesting S&P Profits
strategies.notes: Named but not detailed. Included for completeness.

strategies.name: Dividend Capture Arbitrage
entry_rules: Buy stock before ex-dividend date
Simultaneously hedge with options or paired positions to eliminate directional risk
Capture the dividend payment while remaining market-neutral
Requires very low transaction costs to be profitable
exit_rules: Close position after ex-dividend date
Unwind hedge simultaneously
risk_management: Fully hedged - no directional exposure
Risk comes from execution and transaction costs
Never make a bet you can't afford to lose (mentor Elliot Wolk's rule)
strategies.source_chapter: Alphonse Fletcher Jr. - The Flexibility of True Genius
strategies.notes: Fletcher's early strategy. Simple concept but requires institutional-level transaction costs to be viable. Fletcher evolved from this to more complex structured deals. His key principle: opportunities arise when market participants are treated differently from each other.

strategies.name: Deep Value Bargain Hunter
entry_rules: Stock must have declined 60-70%+ from highs
P/E ratio under 12
Price near or below book value
Growing earnings, revenue, and cash flow (despite price decline)
Significant insider buying (compare purchase amount to buyer's salary - must exceed annual salary)
Verify insider buying is actual share purchase, NOT option exercises
Sell out-of-the-money puts at desired buy price as entry mechanism
exit_rules: Profit target: 20-25% gain
Patiently wait for value to be recognized
Upgrade to better opportunities when available
risk_management: Maximum 10 concentrated positions
Extreme patience: willing to miss 80% of stocks to get the right price
Will sit in cash (as low as 13% invested) when no bargains exist
Put-selling strategy provides income while waiting AND lowers cost basis if filled
strategies.source_chapter: Ahmet Okumus - From Istanbul to the Bull's Eye
strategies.notes: Extremely codifiable with clear quantitative screens. 90%+ win rate reported (likely due to extreme selectivity). The put-selling entry mechanism is a brilliant addition - you get paid while waiting for your price. Key insight: insider buying must be real purchases, not option exercises. Maximum concentration (10 positions) because 'my top ten ideas will always perform better than my top hundred.'

strategies.name: SEPA Momentum - Price Action Sandwich
entry_rules: Long-term screen: Stock must have highest relative strength (top performers in the market)
Fundamental confirmation: Above-average P/E (growth stocks), newer companies, price above $20
Short-term entry: Proprietary chart patterns (~20 patterns developed through personal research)
Stock should be difficult to buy (fills above limit price shows strong demand)
Stock must be in a confirmed uptrend
exit_rules: Maximum loss per trade: ~10% (absolute cap, no exceptions)
Have a re-entry plan if stopped out (don't abandon winners just because you got shaken out)
Partial profit-taking allowed (you don't have to make all-or-nothing decisions)
Trail stops as position moves in your favor
risk_management: Loss capping at fixed maximum level is THE most important rule
50% win rate acceptable if winners are much larger than losers
Risk control is 90% of the battle regardless of strategy
Never hold a losing position out of concern about looking stupid
Analyze all past trades to find patterns in your errors
strategies.source_chapter: Mark Minervini - Stock Trader
strategies.notes: Minervini's 'SEPA' (Specific Entry Point Analysis) is described in concept but proprietary chart patterns are not disclosed. The framework is clear: long-term relative strength screen, fundamental growth confirmation, then short-term technical entry. Key insight: 'Concentrate on mastering one style that suits your personality.' Lost everything initially buying cheap stocks making new lows - the exact opposite of what eventually made him successful.

strategies.name: Mutual Fund Timing System
entry_rules: Computer-generated signals based on proprietary systems
In the market approximately 50-55% of the time
Multiple systems running simultaneously
Specific entry criteria not disclosed
exit_rules: System-generated exit signals
Specific exit criteria not disclosed
risk_management: Out of market 45-50% of the time (huge risk reduction)
Maximum drawdown: ~3% (extraordinary)
Systems have limited lifespans - constantly develop replacements
Never miss a market day (even on day of knee surgery)
Occasionally increase leverage on highest-probability setups (never lost on these)
strategies.source_chapter: Steve Lescarbeau - The Unpredictable Expert
strategies.notes: Lescarbeau refused to reveal any specifics. Reported 70%+ average annual returns with 3% max drawdown - extraordinary if true. Key philosophical insights: (1) Never make predictions, (2) Systems have finite lifespans - always research replacements, (3) Shorting stocks is dumb because the long-term upward bias works against you. The only strategy details: mutual fund timing, in market ~50% of time, computer-based.

strategies.name: Catalyst-Driven Multi-Factor Model
entry_rules: Identify catalyst: earnings surprises, same-store sales, management changes, sector events, product launches
Stack multiple probability-enhancing factors (no single factor is sufficient)
Factors compiled from years of hand-written observations on business cards
Key patterns: how stocks respond to specific types of catalysts
Earnings surprise patterns: direction, magnitude, sector response
exit_rules: Time stop: exit if position hasn't performed within defined time window (typically 2-4 weeks)
Catalyst has played out (event has passed)
Position fails to respond as expected to catalyst
risk_management: Average net exposure ~40% long (significant hedging)
70%+ win rate reported
Use time stops to prevent dead money
Write down lessons from every trade
Multiple factors must align (never trade on single factor)
strategies.source_chapter: Michael Masters - Swimming with the Current
strategies.notes: Masters built his entire model from notes jotted on business cards over years. The concept of multi-factor probability stacking is powerful and codifiable. Time stops are a key differentiator. Key insight: individual factors may have small edges, but combining multiple aligned factors creates high-probability trades. 40% net long average exposure suggests significant risk management through hedging.

strategies.name: Non-Normal Probability Option Trading
entry_rules: Estimate actual probability distribution for specific stock/index (case by case, NOT using Black-Scholes assumptions)
Identify situations where price behavior will be non-random: strong support/resistance, trend-following stop clusters, flow-driven markets
Compare your estimated probability distribution to the market's implied distribution (from option prices)
Buy options that are underpriced relative to YOUR estimated probabilities
Sell options that are overpriced relative to YOUR estimated probabilities
exit_rules: Position hits profit target based on probability estimate
Time decay erodes edge
Probability assessment changes based on new information
risk_management: Each trade is custom-structured based on probability assessment
Hedge positions to isolate the specific probability bet
Trade both US and Japanese markets (works night shifts for Japan)
Reject Black-Scholes assumption that price movements are random/normal
strategies.source_chapter: John Bender - Questioning the Obvious
strategies.notes: Bender's core insight: Black-Scholes assumes random/normal price distributions, but real markets have non-normal distributions due to human behavior (stops, support/resistance, herding). By estimating the ACTUAL probability distribution and comparing it to the ASSUMED distribution in option prices, you find mispriced options. Requires deep understanding of option pricing, probability theory, and market microstructure. Highly intellectual approach but conceptually brilliant.

strategies.name: Acquisition Finance - Structured Equity Deals
entry_rules: Identify consolidator companies with strong CEO track record
Structure preferred equity investment with guaranteed minimum return (10-20%)
CEO must demonstrate superior deal-sourcing ability and operational skill
Negotiate downside protection: if stock drops 40%, receive additional shares
Deal must have 'belt and suspenders' protection - profitable even if stock drops 50%
exit_rules: Hold to maturity of preferred structure
Exit when guaranteed return has been achieved
Exit if CEO track record deteriorates or business fundamentals change
risk_management: Structured deals with contractual downside protection
Preferred equity position (senior to common stock)
Anti-dilution provisions
Philosophy: 'Eliminate the downside. If you eliminate the downside, you end up making money.'
strategies.source_chapter: Claudio Guazzoni - Eliminating the Downside
strategies.notes: Not replicable by retail traders - requires tens of millions in capital and ability to negotiate private deals. However, the PHILOSOPHY is universally applicable: buy assets at deep discounts with structural downside protection. Key insight: 'Every period of time has its own opportunities where you can find investments that are extremely discounted and have a very well protected downside.' Complexity is not necessary for success - his first strategy (buying restricted stock at 50 cents on the dollar) was brilliantly simple.

strategies.name: Restricted Stock Discount Strategy
entry_rules: Identify company insiders holding restricted stock post-IPO
Search SEC insider ownership filings for large holdings
Contact insiders and offer to buy small portion of restricted holdings at 50 cents on the dollar
Take on the restriction period yourself in exchange for the discount
exit_rules: Hold until restriction period expires
Sell on open market once restriction lifts
risk_management: 50% discount provides significant margin of safety
Negotiate additional protection: if stock drops 40%, receive more shares from seller
Only buy small portion of insider holdings (they keep skin in the game)

indicators.name: Restriction period length
indicators.parameters: Was 3 years, now reduced to 1 year

strategies.source_chapter: Claudio Guazzoni - Eliminating the Downside
strategies.notes: No longer viable in original form because SEC reduced restriction period to 1 year, shrinking available discounts. However, the CONCEPT of exploiting structural inefficiencies where some market participants are treated differently remains powerful. A perfect example of finding opportunity in regulatory asymmetry.

strategies.name: Multi-Strategy Statistical Arbitrage
entry_rules: Combine 20+ different predictive techniques (no single technique is sufficient)
Each strategy based on hypothesis-first approach (NOT data mining)
Identify pricing discrepancies between related securities across global markets
Multiple weak signals that are individually unprofitable combine to overcome transaction costs
Use fundamental data (balance sheets, income statements, volume) in addition to price data
exit_rules: Systematic exit when pricing discrepancy closes
Dynamic model adjustment as market conditions change
Drop strategies that cease to work, introduce new ones
risk_management: Balance all long positions with correlated short positions (market-neutral)
Hedge against: market direction, currency exposure, interest rate moves, sector rotation
Minimize exposure to all risk factors you cannot predict
Maintain exposure only to factors where you have predictive ability
Avoid excessive leverage (lesson from LTCM collapse)
strategies.source_chapter: David Shaw - The Quantitative Edge
strategies.notes: Shaw refused to reveal specifics. Key conceptual insights for any trader: (1) Individual weak signals can combine into strong ones when they agree (synergy principle), (2) Hypothesis-first testing prevents data mining, (3) Easy market inefficiencies are long gone - remaining edges require combining multiple small advantages, (4) Barriers to entry in quantitative trading are very high and increasing. 22% average annual return with 11% max drawdown over 11 years. The synergy principle (marginal indicators becoming useful in combination) is applicable to any trading approach.

strategies.name: Pairs Trading (Statistical Arbitrage)
entry_rules: Define pairs of stocks that historically move together (high correlation)
Monitor each pair for statistically significant performance divergence
When divergence exceeds threshold: sell the outperformer, buy the underperformer
Bet on convergence back to historical relationship
exit_rules: Close when pair relationship converges back to normal
Stop loss if divergence widens beyond acceptable threshold
risk_management: Market neutral by construction (long one stock, short the other)
Diversify across many pairs
Monitor for structural breaks in pair relationships
strategies.source_chapter: David Shaw - The Quantitative Edge (conceptual explanation)
strategies.notes: Described as a conceptual illustration, not Shaw's actual strategy. Referenced a 1999 Yale study (Gatev, Goetzmann, Rouwenhort) showing pairs trading was profitable 1963-1993 but deteriorated significantly 1994-1997 as more firms adopted it. Schwager notes this is likely much simpler than what Shaw actually does. Important warning: strategies that work can stop working when they become popular.

strategies.name: Tape Reading / Intuitive Trading
entry_rules: 50% gut feel based on tape reading (price action, volume, order flow)
Trade off tape action, sector rotation, or specific catalysts (three different trigger types)
Require a good reason for every trade (never short just because 'stock is too high')
40% of stock movement = market, 30% = sector, 30% = stock-specific
Place limit orders away from market before major announcements to capture overreactions
exit_rules: If catalyst is negated, exit immediately (IBM earnings example: covered at $187 same night, opened $197 next day)
If wrong and don't know why: take off half immediately, can always re-enter
Do this twice and you've reduced 75% of position painlessly
Let the market tell you if your theory is right, don't fight it
risk_management: 55% win rate is normal for best traders (63% is exceptional)
Make sure losses are smaller than winners
Never take position too big relative to portfolio (common mistake)
Diversify by sector with specialized traders covering each area
5% of trades account for virtually all profits - know when to bet big

indicators.name: Market breadth
indicators.parameters: Rally participation (big caps only vs broad)

strategies.source_chapter: Steve Cohen - The Trading Room
strategies.notes: Cohen's edge cannot be taught - it is a distillation of tens of thousands of trades into intuitive pattern recognition. 45% annual return (90% gross before 50% performance fee). Key codifiable lessons: (1) Always have a catalyst/reason, (2) Cut losses reflexively, (3) Take half off when uncertain, (4) 5% of trades make all the money - sizing matters, (5) Match trading style to personality, (6) Pair traders with analysts for sector expertise. Employed psychiatrist Ari Kiev full-time to optimize trader psychology.

strategies.name: Sector Catalyst Short Selling
entry_rules: Identify stock in weak sector that is rising for artificial reasons (e.g., index fund buying, fad product)
Wait for the artificial buying catalyst to expire (e.g., index rebalance complete, IPO lockup)
Position short just before catalyst expiration (2-3 days before)
Must have a specific, time-bound catalyst for the trade
exit_rules: Cover if catalyst event passes and stock does not decline
Take profits on sharp decline
Cover immediately if thesis is wrong
risk_management: Short-term trades with defined catalyst and time horizon
Never hold shorts without a catalyst
Don't fight momentum without a reason
strategies.source_chapter: Steve Cohen - The Trading Room
strategies.notes: Cohen's example: shorted 1 million shares of a stock in a weak sector that was rising only due to S&P index addition buying. Once index fund buying completed, stock collapsed. Stock also reported disappointing earnings, turning it into a home run. The key principle: know WHY a stock is moving and whether that reason is temporary.

strategies.name: Out-of-the-Money Put Selling as Entry Mechanism
entry_rules: Identify stock you want to buy at a lower price using deep value criteria
Sell out-of-the-money puts at your desired purchase price (strike = target entry)
Collect premium while waiting for stock to decline to your price
exit_rules: If stock stays above strike: puts expire worthless, keep premium as pure profit
If stock falls below strike: you are assigned the stock at your target price, MINUS the premium collected (lower effective cost basis)
Either outcome is acceptable by design
risk_management: Only sell puts on stocks you genuinely want to own at the strike price
Stock must pass all deep value screens first
Downside risk: stock could fall well below strike, but you wanted to own it there anyway
Premium income reduces cost basis and provides income during periods of no action

indicators.name: Same as Deep Value Bargain Hunter (SMW-010)
indicators.parameters: All value screens must pass first

strategies.source_chapter: Ahmet Okumus - From Istanbul to the Bull's Eye
strategies.notes: Brilliant enhancement to value investing. Instead of placing a limit buy order at your target price (which earns nothing while waiting), sell puts at that price and collect income. If filled, your cost basis is lower. If not filled, you earned pure premium. Win-win by design. Highly replicable by any investor with options approval. Schwager highlights this as an approach 'that could be very useful to many investors but is probably utilized by very few.'

strategies.name: Event Overreaction Fade
entry_rules: Before major announcement (Fed decision, earnings, economic data), place limit orders well away from current price in BOTH directions
If market overreacts in either direction, your limit order gets filled (fading the extreme)
The orders capture 'something stupid' the market might do in the heat of the moment
exit_rules: Quick reversal trade - exit once initial overreaction subsides
If the move is fundamentally justified (not overreaction), cut quickly
risk_management: Orders placed at extreme levels so only filled on genuine overreaction
Small position size relative to portfolio
Must be able to distinguish overreaction from genuine repricing

indicators.name: Economic calendar
indicators.parameters: Fed announcements, earnings dates, data releases

strategies.source_chapter: Steve Cohen - The Trading Room
strategies.notes: Observed during the interview: Cohen placed buy and sell orders well away from market before a Fed announcement, explaining 'in case the market does something stupid.' This is a defined, repeatable approach to capturing post-announcement overreactions. Requires fast execution and willingness to be wrong sometimes.

universal_principles: Discipline is the only trait shared by ALL successful traders
There is no single right way to trade - methods must match personality
Risk control is more important than entry strategy (90% of the battle per Minervini)
Confidence appears to be an inherent trait of successful traders, not just a result of success
Hard work and obsessiveness are common traits among market wizards
Successful traders are innovators, not followers - they develop original approaches
All traders should keep detailed records of observations and trade lessons
Catalysts are essential - value alone is not sufficient reason to buy
When market behavior contradicts your thesis, exit immediately
Great traders continuously evolve and adapt to changing markets
5% of trades often account for nearly all profits - knowing when to bet big is crucial
Past performance means nothing if conditions have changed
Popularity destroys sound strategies (portfolio insurance 1987, pairs trading 1990s)
Data mining without hypothesis testing produces worthless trading systems
Short selling reduces portfolio risk when viewed as a hedge, not a standalone trade
key_risk_management_rules: Never make a bet you can't afford to lose (Fletcher/Wolk)
Take off half when uncertain, can always re-enter (Cohen)
Cap losses at a fixed maximum (Minervini: ~10%)
Use time stops to avoid dead money (Masters, Cook)
Short positions MUST have stop losses due to unlimited risk
Never use excessive leverage (Lauer 1987, Cook early career, LTCM)
Stop trading during personal difficulties (Cook, Walton)
most_codifiable_strategies: SMW-002: Lauer's Six-Screen Deep Value Process (clarity: 9, completeness: 9)
SMW-010: Okumus Deep Value Bargain Hunter (clarity: 9, completeness: 9)
SMW-021: Okumus Put-Selling Entry Mechanism (clarity: 9, completeness: 9)
SMW-004: Galante Short Selling Framework (clarity: 8, completeness: 8)
SMW-013: Masters Catalyst Multi-Factor Model (clarity: 6, completeness: 6)

high: Deep value with catalyst (Lauer, Okumus) - timeless approach
Short selling red flags (Galante) - accounting fraud still exists
Put-selling as entry mechanism (Okumus) - works in any market
Catalyst-driven trading (Cohen, Masters) - events still move stocks
Risk management principles - universally applicable forever
medium: Momentum/relative strength (Minervini) - works in trending markets
Options mispricing via probability estimation (Bender) - still viable with skill
Cumulative tick day trading (Cook) - concept valid, specific levels may have shifted
Event overreaction fading (Cohen) - algo competition has increased
low: Pairs trading (Shaw description) - largely arbitraged away by quant firms
Dividend capture arbitrage (Fletcher) - margins too thin for retail
Restricted stock discount (Guazzoni) - SEC rule changes eliminated opportunity
Mutual fund timing (Lescarbeau) - most funds now have short-term redemption fees
